coredata.dc:title: Regulating the overlap between the absorption spectrum of metal ion-chromogenic agent and the emission spectrum of carbon-based dots to improve the sensing performance for metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yingmei
Shang, Pengxiang
Dong, Yongqiang
Chi, Yuwu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based dots (CDs) exhibiting excellent fluorescence (FL) properties have been well applied in sensing. In particular, many FL sensors based CDs have been developed for various metal ions. However, the sensors based on the CDs without special functionalization usually show poor selectivity and sensitivity. In this work, cobalt(II) ion (Co2+) is taken as the model to introduce a novel, sensitive and selective FL sensing mode for metal ions based on CDs without special functionalization. A kind of highly luminescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) are used as the probes. The FL signal of N,S-CDs is insensitive to Co2+ itself, but is quenched sensitively and selectively by Co2+ in the presence of cysteamine. Actually, cysteamine reacts with Co2+ to form a positively charged and absorbent complex (Co(cys)3 2+), whose absorption band covers completely the emission spectrum of N,S-CDs. Then, a FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) system is formed, in which N,S-CDs act as the donor and Co(cys)3 2+ acts as the acceptor. Furthermore, the electrostatic interaction between the positivity charged Co(cys)3 2+ and the negatively charged N,S-CDs increases the collision, enhancing the FRET effects. As a result, the FL signal of N,S-CDs can be dynamically quenched by Co(cys)3 2+. Finally, a sensitive and selective FL sensor is developed for Co2+. More importantly, this paper provides CDs a novel FL sensing mode, which may be applied in many other metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based dots
Fluorescence
Collisional quenching
Cobalt(II) ion
Sensing
Cysteamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of FL sensor for Co2+ based on the FL of N,S-CDs
Establishment of the FL sensor for Co(II) ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: The Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675026, 21305017, 21375020, 21675027), National Basic Research Program of China (2010CB732400), and the 1Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT1116). The authors thank Professor Xinqi Zhang at Instrumentation Analysis and Measurement Center of Fuzhou University for the help in TEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs quenched by Co(cys)3 2+.

FL spectra of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs in the presence of Co2+ ions, cysteamine, or cysteamine with Co2+ ions; UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs, Co2+ ions, cysteamine, and cysteamine+Co2+ ions in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.02M, pH 7.4). The inset shows the photos of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs in the presence of Co2+ ions, cysteamine, or cysteamine with Co2+ ions under the excitation of 365nm UV light. Concentration of N,S-CDs: 40μgmL−1; concentration of Co2+ ions: 0.1mM; concentration of cysteamine: 2mM.

Effect of pH value on the FL responses of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs+cysteamine, and N,S-CDs+cysteamine+Co2+ ions in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.02M, pH 7.4). Concen-tration of N,S-CDs: 40μgmL−1; concentration of Co2+ ions: 0.1mM; concentration of cysteamine: 2mM.

FL responses of N,S-CDs towards cysteamine of different concentrations in the absence and presence of Co2+ ions in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.02M, pH 7.4). Concentration of N,S-CDs: 40μgmL−1; concentration of Co2+ ions: 0.1mM.

FL response of the N,S-CDs towards Co2+ ions and other positive ions in the absence and presence of cysteamine. Concentration of N,S-CDs: 40μgmL−1; concentration of cysteamine: 2mM; concentration of Co2+ and other positive ions: 0.1mM; buffer solution: 0.02M Tris-HCl of pH 7.4.

FL responses of the sensor towards Co2+ ions of different concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.08, 0.40, 0.80, 4.00, 6.00, 8.00, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0, 80.0, 100μM). Concentration of N,S-CDs: 40μgmL−1; concentration of cysteamine: 2mM; buffer solution: 0.02M Tris-HCl of pH 7.4.

head.ce:title: Regulating the overlap between the absorption spectrum of metal ion-chromogenic agent and the emission spectrum of carbon-based dots to improve the sensing performance for metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt(II) ion is taken as the model to introduce a novel, sensitive and selective fluorescence sensing mode for metal ions based on carbon-based dots without special functionalization.

Carbon-based dots (CDs) exhibiting excellent fluorescence (FL) properties have been well applied in sensing. In particular, many FL sensors based CDs have been developed for various metal ions. However, the sensors based on the CDs without special functionalization usually show poor selectivity and sensitivity. In this work, cobalt(II) ion (Co2+) is taken as the model to introduce a novel, sensitive and selective FL sensing mode for metal ions based on CDs without special functionalization. A kind of highly luminescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) are used as the probes. The FL signal of N,S-CDs is insensitive to Co2+ itself, but is quenched sensitively and selectively by Co2+ in the presence of cysteamine. Actually, cysteamine reacts with Co2+ to form a positively charged and absorbent complex (Co(cys)3 2+), whose absorption band covers completely the emission spectrum of N,S-CDs. Then, a FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) system is formed, in which N,S-CDs act as the donor and Co(cys)3 2+ acts as the acceptor. Furthermore, the electrostatic interaction between the positivity charged Co(cys)3 2+ and the negatively charged N,S-CDs increases the collision, enhancing the FRET effects. As a result, the FL signal of N,S-CDs can be dynamically quenched by Co(cys)3 2+. Finally, a sensitive and selective FL sensor is developed for Co2+. More importantly, this paper provides CDs a novel FL sensing mode, which may be applied in many other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based dots (CDs) are emerging zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, which are usually composed of sp2 carbon cores and surface functional groups [1,2]. Due to the quantum confinement and edge effects, CDs exhibit many outstanding optical properties such as fluorescence (FL), chemiluminescence (CL) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [3,4]. Furthermore, compared with traditional luminescent semiconductor quantum dots, CDs usually show good water solubility, non/low toxicity, environmental friendly, excellent biocompatibility, and robust chemical inertness [5,6]. Accordingly, CDs became promising candidates for various exciting applications and have been well used in sensor based on their optical properties [4]. In particular, a lot of PL sensors have been developed for various metal ions. Unfortunately, most of the CDs without any special functionalization usually show relative poor selectivity toward the analyte due to the non-specific interactions between CDs and metal ions [7–9]. To improve the selectivity, CDs were usually surface-modified with some special functional groups or molecules which can selectively capture some metal ions [10–12]. However, the functionalization of CDs is usually difficult. As a result, the kinds of functionalized CDs are quite few till now, limiting seriously the wide applications of CDs in sensing numerous metal ions. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to develop a new mode to extend the application of CDs.

Most of the present highly luminescent CDs are blue-emissive and negatively charged. The FL signal can be dynamically quenched by some positively charged and absorbent target molecules, whose absorption band overlaps with the emission spectrun of CDs, due to the electrostatic interaction and FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) effects [13]. Actually, almost metal ions are positively charged. However, most metal ions show no or low absorption in the wavelength range corresponding to the emission spectrum of CDs. Therefore, the FL signal can’t be efficiently quenched by metal ions. It has been well studied that many metal ions can be selectively captured by some special organic ligands to form strong absorbent complexes [14–17]. Furthermore, the formed complexes are usually positively charged. Thus, FRET systems can be established, in which the formed absorbent complexes are acceptors while CDs act as energy donors. As a result, the metal ions can be detected by determining the quenched FL signal.

Herein, cobalt (II) ion (Co2+) is chosen as a model to describe the novel FL sensing mode for metal ions. As an essential metal ion, Co2+ is a key component of vitamin B12, whose active center of coenzymes called cobalamins, stimulating the production of red blood cells [18–20]. Although Co2+ is essential for humans, higher doses are toxic. The heavy discharge of Co2+ ions into the environment may jeopardize health and result in diseases [21–24]. Thus, interest in the environmental and biological monitoring of Co2+ has increased. Although numerous methods have been demonstrated for the quantification of Co2+, most of the methods are limited by the complex operation, low sensitivity and/or expensive equipment. Therefore, it is desirable to develop a simple, rapid, sensitive and low-cost method for trace Co2+ ions monitoring. In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) of high FL and negatively charged are used as the FL probe to detect Co2+ in the presence of cysteamine [25], which can react with Co2+ to form an absorbent and positively charged complex (Co(cys)3 2+) [26–28]. Then, the bright blue FL signal of the N,S-CDs can be quenched sensitively through a dynamic collisional quenching mechanism. The developed FL sensing system shows good selectivity and high sensitivity, allowing a green, simple and reliable detection of Co2+ ions in environmental samples.

Citric acid monohydrate (99.9%), l-cysteine (97.0%), cysteamine (95%), cobaltous chloride hexahydrate (99.99%) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment. Tris-HCl buffer solution of different pH values were prepared by titrating 0.02M Tris solution with a concentrated hydrochloric acid solution (6M) to the required pH values.

N,S-CDs were prepared by hydrothermally treating the mixture of citric acid and l-cysteine as described previously [25]. For a typical synthesis procedure, 2g of citric acid monohydrate and 1g of l-cysteine were dissolved in 5mL DI water, followed by drying at 70°C for 12h. The resulted viscous syrup was heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200°C for 3h. The obtained black syrup product was dissolved in 20mL DI water and neutralized with 1M NaOH solution. Finally, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1kDa) against DI water for 72h to remove the residual organics.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed on an electronic microscope (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200KV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured on an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). FL spectra of the prepared N,S-CDs were recorded by an FL spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse Varian). UV/vis absorption spectra were carried out on a UV/vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). An Agilent 7500 ICPMS system (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA) was applied to detect Co2+ concentration in a Min River water sample.

Firstly, 40μL of 1mgmL−1 N,S-CDs and 10μL of 200mM cysteamine were placed in a 2mL colorimetric tube, followed by the addition of 940μL Tris-HCl buffer solution. Then, 10μL Co2+ of different concentrations was added into the mixture. The solution was incubated at room temperature for 1h before the FL measurement (except for the experiment of optimazing reaction time). A reagent blank experiment was carried out under the same experiment condition.

The water sample collected from Min River (Fujian, China) was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane (Millipore) before the measurement. Aliquots (500μL) of the filtered water sample spiked with standard Co2+ ion solutions (10μL, final concentration 10–100μM) were diluted to 1000μL with Tris-HCl (0.02M, pH 7.4) containing N,S-CDs and cysteamine (the final concentrations of N,S-CDs and cysteamine were 40μgmL−1 and 2mM, respectively). Then the FL intensities of the spiked solutions were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of FL sensor for Co2+ based on the FL of N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The HRTEM image (Fig. 1 ) indicates that the used N,S-CDs are nanosheets (with typical lattice constant 0.245nm) of graphene. The lateral sizes of the N,S-CDs are mainly distributed in the range from 4 to 8nm, while the heights mainly range from 0.5 to 1.5nm. Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectrum of the obtained N,S-CDs. The result confirms that the as-obtained N,S-CDs contain both sp2 carbon structures (ν(CC) at 1545cm−1) and oxygen-containing functional groups (ν(OH) at 3450cm−1, ν(NH) at 3100cm−1, ν(CO) at 1720cm−1, ν as(COO−) at 1640cm−1 and ν s(COO−) at 1400cm−1). The aqueous solution of N,S-CDs shows excitation-dependent FL emission (Fig. S1). It shows the strongest FL signal under the excitation of 345nm, with the maximum emission wavelength centered at around 420nm. Under the irradiation of 365nm UV light, the solution exhibits bright blue FL emission.

The blue FL of the N,S-CD solution is quenched sig-nificantly by Co2+ ions in the presence of cysteamine (see the inset of Fig. 3 ). The FL spectra indicate that the FL intensity at 420nm has decreased by ∼68%. However, the control experiments indicate that either Co2+ ions or cysteamine can only quench slightly the FL of N,S-CDs (less than 5%). The UV–vis absorption spectra indicate that cysteamine has no absorption above 250nm, while Co2+ ions produce absorption only when the wavelength is below 320nm. Furthermore, neither cysteamine nor Co2+ ions can react with the N,S-CDs to produce any new absorption band (Fig. S2). However, as Co2+ ions and cysteamine are co-present in the solution, obvious absorption bands can be observed in the range below 650nm, covering the whole wavelength range of N,S-CDs’ absorption and emission bands. The strong absorption band implies that Co2+ react with cysteamine to form a chelate complex (Co(cys)3 2+), which has been reported [26–28]. The FL quenching should be related to the formed Co(cys)3 2+. It can be seen that the co-presenting Co2+ ions, cysteamine and N,S-CDs exhibit absorption bands simply overlapping those of Co(cys)3 2+ and N,S-CDs, without any new absorption band. The result implies that Co(cys)3 2+ may quench the FL signal of N,S-CDs through a dynamic way rather than a static one, which may be further proved in the following experiments.

The concentration of N,S-CDs affects obviously both F0 and F, where F0 is the FL intensity of N,S-CDs in the presence of cysteamine but absence of Co2+ ions, and F is the FL intensity of N,S-CDs in the presence of both cysteamine and Co2+ ions. As shown in Fig. S3, both F0 and F increase with the concentration of N,S-CDs below 40μgmL−1, then the intensities have no obvious enhancement when the concentration of N,S-CDs is further increased. Different form F0 and F, the quenching efficiency (F0 /F) is nearly independent on the con-centration of N,S-CDs (see the inset of Fig. S3). However, the quenching efficiencies increase with the increasing temperature due to the increase of diffusion and collisions between quencher and the N,S-CDs (Fig. S4). The experimental results further confirm that Co(cys)3 2+ should quench the FL signal of N,S-CDs through a dynamic collisional quenching mechanism.

Herein, a collisional quenching mechanism can be proposed for the FL response of N,S-CDs towards Co2+ ions in the presence of cysteamine. As shown in Scheme 1 , Co2+ ions react with cysteamine to form a strong absorbent and positively charged Co(cys)3 2+, which then collides with the negatively charged N,S-CDs due to the electrostatic interaction. As a result, the energy transfers from the excited N,S-CDs to the Co(cys)3 2+, leading to the FL quenching of the N,S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the FL sensor for Co(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: As has been discussed above, Co2+ ions can quench effectively the FL emission of N,S-CDs in the presence of cysteamine. Accordingly, a sensitive sensor can be developed for the detection of Co2+ ions based on the quenching effect. Subsequently, experimental conditions including the pH value of solution, the response time, and the concentrations of N,S-CDs and cysteamine are optimized before evaluating the performance of the sensor.

The pH value of solution is a key factor to affect the performance of the sensor. As shown in Fig. 4 , in the pH range from 5 to 9, the FL intensity of the N,S-CDs in the absence of cysteamine is nearly independent on the pH value of the solution. However, in the presence of cysteamine, the FL intensity decreases slightly as the pH value is increased. When the Co2+ ions are further added into the solution, the FL quenching efficiency increases obviously with the increase of pH value from 5 to 7.4, then keeps stable when the pH value is further increased. The pH dependent quenching effect may be relate to the abundant carboxyl groups on N,S-CDs. As has been discussed that the FL signal of N,S-CDs are quenched dynamically by the positively charged Co(cys)3 2+. Alkaline solutions are helpful for the ionization of carboxyl groups, increasing the electronegativity of the N,S-CDs. Then, the collision opportunity between N,S-CDs and Co(cys)3 2+ increases, leading to higher quenching efficiency. Accordingly, Tris-HCl buffer solution of pH 7.4 is applied in the sensor.

The response rate of the sensor to Co2+ ions is subsequently investigated by monitoring the time-dependent FL changes upon addition of 0.1mM Co2+ ions. As shown in Fig. S5, the FL intensity at 420nm is quenched by ∼70% within 1min, and the FL intensity decreases to the minimum value in 1h, then remains stable in the following 0.5h observation. Accordingly, the quenched FL intensity should be measured at 1h after the Co2+ ions are added into the solution.

Although the concentration of N,S-CDs has nearly no effect on the quenching efficiency as discussed above, it is the dominant factor to affect the FL intensity of the sensor. Accordingly, it affects the relative standard deviation (RSD) of both F0 and F, which may further affect the RSD of the quenching efficiency and detection limit of the sensor. It can be seen from the inset in Fig. S3, the RSD of quenching efficiency decreases as the concentration of N,S-CDs is increased from 0.6 to 40μgmL−1, but has no obvious decrease when the concentration of N,S-CDs is further increased. Therefore, 40μgmL−1 N,S-CDs should be applied in the sensor for obtaining the highest accuracy and the lowest detection limit, which has been discussed elsewhere [29].

As discussed above, cysteamine is necessary for the sensor. Apparently, the concentration of cysteamine is a key factor to affect the performance of the sensor. As shown in Fig. 5 , when cysteamine is lower than 2mM, the FL signal of N,S-CDs can’t be efficiently quenched due to the fact that cysteamine at low concentrations is insufficient to react with all Co2+ ions into Co(cys)3 2+. However, when the concentration of cysteamine is higher than 2mM, cysteamine quenches obviously the FL signal of N,S-CDs even in the absence of Co2+ ions. Furthermore, the quenching effect increases with the increasing concentration of cysteamine. Therefore, 2mM cysteamine is chosen for further study.

To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system for Co2+ ions, effects of some other positive ions (Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Sn2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Na+, Al3+, Cu2+, K+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Li+, Cd2+) on the FL response of N,S-CDs either in the presence or absence of cysteamine are investigated. The counter anions were NO3 − or Cl−, which are proved to have no obvious effect on the FL of N,S-CDs. The UV–vis spectra indicate that most of the investigated ions have nearly no absorption above 360nm even in the presence or absence of cysteamine (Fig. S6). Only Cr3+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ produce absorption above 360nm (Fig. S7). However, the absorption bands are much weaker than that of Co(cys)3 2+. In other words, most of the ions are not able to provide efficient acceptor to absorb the energy of excited N,S-CDs, even in the presence of cysteamine. As a result, most of the ions have no obvious effect on the FL signal of N,S-CDs no matter the cysteamine is present or not (Fig. 6 ).

The dynamic response range and detection limit of the sensing system were measured under the optimum experimental conditions discussed above. As shown in Fig. 7 , the sensing system is sensitive towards Co2+ ions. The FL intensity decreases obviously as the concentration of Co2+ is increased from 0.08 to 100μM. A good semilogarithmic correlation between the quenching efficiency and the concentration of Co2+ ions can be observed (see inset of Fig. 7 and Eq. (1)):
(1) Log(F0 /F)=5120.3C −0.0046
R2 =0.9955

where C is the concentration of Co2+ ions. The detection limit (3 times of the standard deviation of 10 parallel blank measurements) is detected to be about 80nM (or equivalently 4.72ngmL−1). Compared with the other previously reported results of using different fluorescent probes to detect Co2+, our method exhibited a better linear range and detection limit (Table S1). These results show that the developed sensor have very promising application in the detection of Co2+.

To demonstrate the applicability, the developed sensor is applied to detect the Co2+ ions in a river water sample. The experimental result indicates that the sensor produces no detectable FL response to the river water. That is to say, the concentration of Co2+ ions is lower than the lowest detectable concentration of the method, which is proved by the fact that the concentration of Co2+ ions in the sample is detected to be only 1nM by the ICPMS method. Thus the river water sample was spiked with 1.7μM Co2+ ions, the maximum contaminant level (0.1ppm) of groundwater quality standard for Co2+ according to the New Jersey Ground Water Quality Standards rules [30]. The recovery is calculated to be 104% (Fig. S8), implying the good applicability of the FL sensor in environmental sensing.

In summary, a sensitive and selective sensing system has been developed for the detection of Co2+ ions. In the system, blue emissive N,S-CDs without any special functionalization are used as the FL probes, while the cysteamin is used to react with Co2+ ions to form a positively charged and absorbent Co(cys)3 2+ complex. The collision between the negatively charged N,S-CDs and the positively charged Co(cys)3 2+ leads to a FL quenching. The developed FL sensor exhibits many advantages such as low cost, simple operation, rapid detection, and good environmental friendliness. Thus the sensing method is envisioned as an effective alternative to those traditional methods, such as inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry, potentiometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, electrochemical approaches and fiber optic-linear array detection spectrophotometry. What is more significant, the sensing method provides a novel mode that may be also used in the detection of other metal ions, and further promotes the applications of CDs in sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675026, 21305017, 21375020, 21675027), National Basic Research Program of China (2010CB732400), and the 1Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT1116). The authors thank Professor Xinqi Zhang at Instrumentation Analysis and Measurement Center of Fuzhou University for the help in TEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.102.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yingmei Chen received her BS (2012) from the Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, China. She is currently pursuing her doctorate under the supervision of Prof. Yuwu Chi at Fuzhou University, Fujian, China. Her research interests focus on analytical chemistry, biosensors and carbon nanomaterials.

Pengxiang Shang received his BS (2014) from the Pharmacy Faculty, Hubei University of Chinese Medicine, China. He is currently pursuing his master’s degree under the supervision of Prof. Yuwu Chi at Fuzhou University, Fujian, China. His research interests focus on analytical chemistry and layered nanomaterials.

Yongqiang Dong received his BS (2006) and PhD (2011) degrees from the department of chemistry, Fuzhou University, China. He entered Nanyang Technologic University, Singapore as a postdoctoral researcher from 2011 to 2013. Currently, he is an associate professor of chemistry at Fuzhou University. His research interests mainly relate to the synthesis, luminescent properties and sensing applications of carbon based dots.

Yuwu Chi received his BS (1992) and MS (2000, Prof. Guonan Chen) degrees in Analytical Chemistry at Fuzhou University, and then a Ph D degree in applied physics (2005, Prof. Koichi Aoki) from the University of Fukui. From 1992 to 2002, he worked at Fuzhou University as a research assistant and a lecturer in turn. In 2005, he re-entered Fuzhou University after obtaining his Ph D degree in Japan, and worked as a professor at the College of Chemistry. Presently, his research interests focus on functional carbon materials, electrochemiluminescence, electroanalysis, chemical and biochemical sensing.
